title: Question 1
stem: The distinction between a transudate and an exudate is based on Light criteria and is determinative for the evaluation and treatment of a pleural effusion. What parameter(s) among the listed is / are the Light criteria for drawing this distinction?
teaching_point: . Protein and LDH in the effusion fluid and in the serum. The difference between transudate and exudate are based on modified Light criteria, which includes pleural fluid protein/serum protein ratio of > 0.5, pleural fluid lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)/serum LDH ratio of > 0.6 and pleural fluid LDH > 66% of the upper limits of normal laboratory value for serum LDH. Answers A, B and C are incorrect because analyzing amylase, NTproBNP and pH level in effusion fluid can help in detecting the cause of pleural effusion but these do not help in differentiating between transudate and exudate. Pleural effusion

title: Question 2
stem: Regarding pleural effusion, which of the following is true?
teaching_point: . The international system for reporting serous fluid cytopathology standardized the way to report cytopathology diagnosis of serous fluids, which includes 5 different categories: nondiagnostic (ND), negative for malignancy (NFM), atypia of undetermined significance (AUS), suspicious for malignancy (SFM) and malignant (MAL). the most common causes of pleural effusion are congestive heart failure, cancer, pneumonia and pulmonary embolism. pleural fluid is classified as a transudate or exudate based on modified Light criteria. treatment is based on the underlying condition and whether the effusion is causing severe respiratory symptoms. Pleural effusion

title: Question 7
stem: A 36 year old woman presents with pelvic discomfort, worsening over 6 months. Transvaginal ultrasound shows a multicystic lesion involving the cul de sac, left uterine adnexa and rectal serosa. The lesion is resected by laparoscopy. A representative H&E stained slide is shown. The cyst lining cells are positive for cytokeratin AE1 / AE3, calretinin and WT1 and negative for MOC31. The lesion is extensively sampled and no infiltrative growth is identified. Which of following is true?
teaching_point: Local recurrence affects ~50% of patients. This is a multiloculated peritoneal inclusion cyst. The published literature and WHO blue book indicate a local recurrence rate as high as 50% (answer D). Answer A is incorrect as peritoneal inclusion cysts lack BAP1 mutations; rather, ~70% of peritoneal mesotheliomas harbor BAP1 mutations. peritoneal inclusion cysts lack recurrent copy number alterations. The molecular underpinnings of these lesions remain uncertain. Answer C is incorrect as the potential for local recurrence (particularly in larger multiloculated lesions) warrants clinical follow up. Answer E is incorrect as peritoneal inclusion cysts predominantly affect women, not men. Peritoneal inclusion cyst

title: Question 8
stem: Which of the following statements regarding peritoneal inclusion cysts is true?
teaching_point: Surgical excision is considered the mainstay of treatment. Answer A is incorrect. Although the literature contains rare reports of apparent progression from peritoneal inclusion cyst to malignant mesothelioma, the rate of progression is much lower than 20%. Answer B is incorrect as peritoneal inclusion cysts lack CDKN2A deletions. Instead, ~40 - 50% of pleural mesotheliomas harbor CDKN2A deletion. Answer C is incorrect as ER and PR expression are rarely seen in peritoneal inclusion cysts. Answer D is incorrect as peritoneal inclusion cysts lack destructive invasion of underlying tissues. The presence of destructive invasion is consistent with a diagnosis of mesothelioma. Peritoneal inclusion cyst

title: Question 9
stem: Given the morphology of the above neoplasm involving the pleura, what would be the most useful initial panel of stains for diagnosis?
teaching_point: WT1, calretinin, claudin 4, MOC31 is the only answer choice that has 2 mesothelioma markers (WT1, calretinin) and 2 adenocarcinoma markers (claudin 4, MOC31). BAP1 is lost in the majority of epithelioid mesotheliomas and is indicative of malignancy but is not a sensitive initial diagnostic marker. GATA3 and p16 are not specific to mesothelioma. ER and CDX2 are specific to certain metastatic adenocarcinomas but not other adenocarcinomas. Mesothelioma versus adenocarcinoma

title: Question 10
stem: Molecular alterations of which of the following genes is least likely to be occur in pleural mesothelioma?
teaching_point: ALK. Genetic alterations in NF2, CDKN2A and BAP1 are commonly associated with epithelioid mesothelioma of the pleura. ALK-EML4 translocations are seen in 3 - 7% of lung adenocarcinomas. ALK alterations rarely occur in a subset of peritoneal mesotheliomas. Rare case reports of pleural mesothelioma with ALK translocations have now recently been described and although very uncommon are important to recognize because they may impact treatment options. (JCO Precis Oncol 2019;3:PO.19.00048, Transl Lung Cancer Res 2018;7:537) Mesothelioma versus adenocarcinoma

title: Question 11
stem: What is the role of fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) in diagnosing mesothelioma in situ?
teaching_point: . Identifies CDKN2A deletion. In the context of PMIS, FISH plays a crucial role in identifying homozygous deletion of the CDKN2A gene, a tumor suppressor gene located on chromosome 9p21.3. Homozygous deletion of CDKN2A is a frequent genetic alteration in PMIS and is considered a diagnostic criterion for this preinvasive lesion. EGFR amplification is more commonly associated with lung adenocarcinoma and is not a typical finding in PMIS. ALK rearrangements are characteristic of a subset of lung adenocarcinomas and are not relevant to the diagnosis of PMIS. KRAS mutations are more frequently observed in colorectal and lung adenocarcinomas and are not a primary diagnostic feature of PMIS. Mesothelioma in situ (pleura)

title: Question 12
stem: A 74 year old man with a history of asbestos exposure presents with pleural effusion. A pleural biopsy reveals atypical cells confined to the surface without stromal invasion. Which immunohistochemical panel would be most appropriate to confirm mesothelial origin and neoplastic process?
teaching_point: . Calretinin, CK5/6, BAP1. Calretinin is a highly sensitive marker for mesothelial cells, helping to confirm the origin of the atypical cells. CK5/6 is also usually expressed in mesothelial cells, which supports the mesothelial origin in this scenario. Loss of nuclear BAP1 expression is seen in mesothelioma and can be a helpful marker in differentiating mesothelioma from reactive mesothelial proliferations (100% specificity). while calretinin, WT1 and CK8/18 markers can be positive in mesothelioma, they lack the specificity of the combination in answer A and cannot prove malignancy. WT1 can be expressed in a variety of tumors and CK8/18 can be seen in reactive mesothelial cells. this panel is more suggestive of lung adenocarcinoma. TTF1 is a marker of lung and thyroid origin while claudin4 and CK7 can be expressed in lung adenocarcinoma. this panel is not specific for mesothelial differentiation. As mentioned, WT1 can be seen in various tumors, TTF1 points towards lung or thyroid origin and while D2-40 can stain mesothelial proliferation, the use of it in this panel does not offer diagnostic specificity. Mesothelioma in situ (pleura)

title: Question 17
stem: Which of the following statements is true about localized mesothelioma?
teaching_point: Its histopathologic features are identical to those of diffuse mesothelioma. As such, its diagnosis requires correlation with radiologic, intraoperative and gross findings to ascertain that the tumor is indeed solitary with no diffuse serosal involvement. radiologic and intraoperative correlation is needed to render the diagnosis of localized mesothelioma. localized mesothelioma can involve the pleura and other serosal membranes including the peritoneum. the loss of BAP1 nuclear staining is seen in only a subset of localized mesotheliomas. title: Question 18
stem: What is the expected immunohistochemical staining profile for endosalpingiosis?
teaching_point: WT1+, PAX8+, calretinin-, BCL2+. As endosalpingiosis is composed of tubal type epithelium and thought to be derived from the Müllerian system, it is not surprising that this lesion is positive for PAX8 and WT1. Calretinin would be expected to stain lesions of mesothelial origin and ovarian theca origin. Studies have shown that BCL2, in addition to new contemporary markers such as FOXJ1 and phospho-SMAD2, are contemporary and promising markers for tubal epithelium and endosalpingiosis (Gynecol Oncol 2014;132:316). Endosalpingiosis

teaching_point: . This patient was likely exposed to asbestos 35 years ago. Unilateral pleural thickening is highly suspicious for malignant pleural mesothelioma. Answer A is false; metastases from germ cell tumors would be usually bilateral. B is false; this patient would likely present with bilateral pleural effusion and possible bilateral pleural thickening. D is false; exposure to coal dust is not a risk factor for malignant pleural mesothelioma. E is false; metastases from breast carcinoma would be usually bilateral. Diffuse malignant mesothelioma

title: Question 20
stem: This patient has a history of ovarian carcinoma. Which would be the most useful panel of immunostains to rule out malignant mesothelioma in this patient?
teaching_point: . Expression of calretinin and CK5 would indicate malignant pleural mesothelioma while PAX8 and pCEA indicate metastatic ovarian carcinoma. Be aware that PAX8 can be positive in a small subset of malignant pleural mesothelioma. Answer B is false; CAM5.2 and WT1 will be positive in both, malignant pleural mesothelioma and metastatic ovarian carcinoma; MOC31 will be expressed in metastatic carcinoma but can be at least focally expressed in malignant pleural mesothelioma. D2-40 can be expressed in serous carcinomas. C is false; although two malignant pleural mesothelioma markers (D2-40, WT1) and two carcinoma markers (pCEA and MOC31), WT1 can be positive in ovarian carcinomas and MOC31 is sometimes at least focally expressed in malignant pleural mesothelioma. D is false; keratin AE1/AE3 and WT1 will be positive in both, malignant pleural mesothelioma and metastatic ovarian carcinoma; MOC31 will be expressed in metastatic carcinoma but can be at least focally expressed in malignant pleural mesothelioma. E is false; these are three carcinoma markers (pCEA, MOC31, BerEP4) and only one malignant pleural mesothelioma marker which also is frequently expressed in ovarian carcinomas. For workup of malignant pleural mesothelioma, at least two carcinoma markers and two mesothelial markers should be used. Diffuse malignant mesothelioma

answer_options.explanation: . Expression of calretinin and CK5 would indicate malignant pleural mesothelioma while PAX8 and pCEA indicate metastatic ovarian carcinoma. Be aware that PAX8 can be positive in a small subset of malignant pleural mesothelioma. Answer B is false; CAM5.2 and WT1 will be positive in both, malignant pleural mesothelioma and metastatic ovarian carcinoma; MOC31 will be expressed in metastatic carcinoma but can be at least focally expressed in malignant pleural mesothelioma. D2-40 can be expressed in serous carcinomas. C is false; although two malignant pleural mesothelioma markers (D2-40, WT1) and two carcinoma markers (pCEA and MOC31), WT1 can be positive in ovarian carcinomas and MOC31 is sometimes at least focally expressed in malignant pleural mesothelioma. D is false; keratin AE1/AE3 and WT1 will be positive in both, malignant pleural mesothelioma and metastatic ovarian carcinoma; MOC31 will be expressed in metastatic carcinoma but can be at least focally expressed in malignant pleural mesothelioma. E is false; these are three carcinoma markers (pCEA, MOC31, BerEP4) and only one malignant pleural mesothelioma marker which also is frequently expressed in ovarian carcinomas. For workup of malignant pleural mesothelioma, at least two carcinoma markers and two mesothelial markers should be used. Diffuse malignant mesothelioma
Incorrect. The correct answer is A. . Expression of calretinin and CK5 would indicate malignant pleural mesothelioma while PAX8 and pCEA...

title: Question 22
stem: A 65 year old patient with previous asbestos exposure undergoes a pleural biopsy and the slide shown above is prepared. Which of the following immunohistochemical stains would be best for ruling in malignancy?
teaching_point: . BAP1 loss. When differentiating malignant mesothelioma from a benign mesothelial proliferation, BAP1 loss proves malignancy because no benign lesion has been shown to lose BAP1. WT1, although 88% specific for epithelioid mesothelioma, does not possess the ability to determine if a tissue is benign or malignant. claudin4, in addition to being virtually always negative for tissue of mesothelial origin, does not possess the ability to determine if a tissue is benign or malignant. CK5/6, although positive in 91% of epithelioid mesotheliomas, does not differentiate between malignant and benign tissue. while pancytokeratin has near 100% sensitivity for detecting tissue of mesothelial origin, it does not differentiate between malignant and benign tissue. Mesothelioma (pleura)-epithelioid

title: Question 23
stem: When differentiating mesothelioma from carcinoma, which one of the following stain panels is in line with best practices?
teaching_point: . Pancytokeratin, calretinin, WT1, claudin4, MOC31. When determining mesothelial or carcinoma tissue of origin, current best practices suggest a panel of broad spectrum cytokeratin (pancytokeratin), 2 mesothelial markers (calretinin and WT1) and 2 carcinoma markers (claudin4 and MOC31). although recent literature suggests HEG1 and claudin4 may be used as the sole markers for tissue of mesothelial and epithelial origin respectively, S100 is neither specific nor sensitive for either. loss of the stains BAP1 and MTAP are used to prove malignant mesothelial tissue and have no role in differentiating mesothelial tissue from carcinoma tissue. all stains mentioned (except AE1) are sensitive / specific for tissue of mesothelial origin. no stain mentioned is sensitive / specific for tissue of mesothelial origin. Mesothelioma (pleura)-epithelioid
